title: Sodus Point Light
summary: Sodus Point Light is a historic lighthouse located in the village of Sodus Point in Wayne County, New York, marking the eastern entrance to Sodus Bay on Lake Ontario. The present limestone tower was constructed in 1870–1871 to replace an earlier 1825 light station and served as a primary navigational aid guiding vessels into the bay. The lighthouse was discontinued in 1901 as pierhead beacons assumed channel-marking functions. Since 1985, the structure has operated as the Sodus Bay Lighthouse Museum.
text:
Sodus Point Light is a historic lighthouse located in the village of Sodus Point in Wayne County, New York, marking the eastern entrance to Sodus Bay on Lake Ontario. The present limestone tower was constructed in 1870–1871 to replace an earlier 1825 light station and served as a primary navigational aid guiding vessels into the bay. The lighthouse was discontinued in 1901 as pierhead beacons assumed channel-marking functions. Since 1985, the structure has operated as the Sodus Bay Lighthouse Museum.

Geography and setting
Sodus Point Light stands on a bluff overlooking Lake Ontario at the eastern head of Sodus Bay, a shallow embayment along the lake’s southern shoreline. The bay’s shifting sandbars and shallow approaches historically posed navigational hazards to vessels entering the harbor, necessitating the establishment of a permanent navigational aid.

Early lighthouse (1824–1870)
Congress authorized a lighthouse at Sodus Bay on May 26, 1824, and the first lighthouse and keeper’s dwelling were constructed in 1825 on land purchased from William Wickham.
By the mid-nineteenth century, the federal government had formalized lighthouse oversight across the Great Lakes. An 1838 Congressional document described structured inspection districts for Northern Lakes lights, including Lake Ontario. A subsequent 1842 report reviewed lighthouse construction standards and administrative reform nationwide, reflecting evolving federal policy affecting Great Lakes stations.
The original 1825 structure eventually deteriorated due to shoreline exposure and was replaced in the 1870s.

Construction of the present tower (1870–1871)
Work on a replacement lighthouse began in August 1870, and the current tower was completed on June 30, 1871.
Constructed of locally quarried limestone, the square tower measures approximately 10 by 10 feet at its base and rises about 50 feet above its foundation. The focal plane of the light is approximately 70 feet above lake level.
The lighthouse was originally equipped with a Fresnel lens, reflecting technological improvements adopted across Great Lakes light stations during the late nineteenth century.
Stone from the earlier 1825 lighthouse was reportedly reused to construct a protective jetty near the site.

Navigation and pier lights
In addition to the shore-based lighthouse, navigational aids at Sodus Bay included pierhead beacons marking the harbor entrance. An 1852 federal engineering report discussed relocating a beacon from the Sodus pier to Grassy Point due to channel conditions and structural considerations, reflecting ongoing federal evaluation of navigation infrastructure.
By the late nineteenth century, pierhead lights increasingly assumed primary channel-marking roles. The Sodus Point Light was discontinued in 1901 as these outer and inner pier lights became the principal aids guiding vessels into Sodus Bay.

Federal harbor oversight (20th century)
Federal involvement in Great Sodus Bay continued after the lighthouse’s deactivation. In 1913, the War Department transmitted a report to Congress examining harbor conditions under authority of the Rivers and Harbors Act of 1912, evaluating channel depth and improvement needs within the bay.

Preservation and museum
The property was transferred to the Town of Sodus in 1984. The Sodus Bay Lighthouse Museum opened on July 4, 1985, operated by the Sodus Bay Historical Society. The original Fresnel lens was returned to the tower in 1988.
